response:
SECTION 1  
there are two tree hollows in a cedar. A squirrel sitting in front of the second hollow said:  
1) There are no nuts in the other hollow,  
2) There is at least one nut in one of the hollows.  

SECTION 2  
Red squirrels always tell the truth, while gray squirrels always lie.  
So, if the squirrel is red, both statements must be true.  
But if the squirrel is gray, both statements must be false.  

SECTION 3  
If the squirrel is red, then the first statement "There are no nuts in the other hollow" would be true, meaning the other hollow has no nuts.  
The second statement "There is at least one nut in one of the hollows" would also be true, meaning at least one hollow has nuts.  
This is consistent with the scenario where one hollow has nuts and the other doesn't.  

SECTION 4  
If the squirrel is gray, then both statements must be false.  
So, the first statement "There are no nuts in the other hollow" would be false, meaning the other hollow has nuts.  
The second statement "There is at least one nut in one of the hollows" would also be false, meaning both hollows have no nuts.  
This is a contradiction, so the squirrel cannot be gray.  

SECTION 5  
Therefore, the squirrel must be red.  
This is the only consistent scenario where both statements are true.  

SECTION 6  
In conclusion, the squirrel is red.
